original: Adrian I begins his reign as Catholic Pope
English East India Company forces capture the Portuguese fort on the island of Hormus in the Persian Gulf, an important trading center on the route to Mughal India
Dutch garrison on Formosa surrenders to Chinese pirates
French KingLouis XIVlimits freedom of religion
Henri d'Aguesseau's 1st appointment as chancellor of France

Silent Sejm session of the Polish–Lithuanian Commonwealth parliament marks the end of Augustus II's attempts to create an absolute monarchy and the start of Russia's influence and control over the Commonwealth
Parliament of Ratisborn accepts Pragmatic Sanctions
Sardinia and Austria sign military alliance Convention of Turin
1st US steamboat patent issued, by Georgia to Briggs & Longstreet
Chinese troops driven out of Vietnam capital Thang Long
US Supreme Court convenes for 1st time (NYC)
France declares war on Great Britain and Netherlands
Patent granted Ralph Hodgson, NY, for oiled silk & linen
The capital of Upper Canada is moved from Newark to York
Dutch King Louis Napoleon accepts metric system
1st insurance company managed by African Americans, The African Insurance Company opens in Philadelphia
Seville, Spain surrenders to the French without a fight
US Population: 7,239,881, African American population: 1,377,808 (19%)
Volcano Mayon on Luzon Philippines erupts killing 1,200
Baltimore College of Dental Surgery, 1st in US, incorporated
1st adhesive postage stamps in US isssued, by Alexander Greig's City Despatch Post company, in New York City
Oldest continuous writer of insurance in America opens, the Mutual Life Insurance Company of New York (MONY)
Theophile Gautier publishes "Hashish Club" about his initiation
Auburn University is chartered as the East Alabama Male College.
1st rabbi to open House of Representatives, Morris Raphall of NYC
Dike breaks in Gelderland, Netherlands
Texas secedes from the Union (which precipitates the American Civil War)
Julia Howe publishes "Battle Hymn of Republic"
-Feb 8th] Battle of Yazoo River, MS
Second Schleswig War begins when Prussian and Austrian forces cross into Schleswig challenging Danish control
JS Rock, 1st African American lawyer to practise in US Supreme Court, admitted to bar
US President Lincoln signs 13th Amendment of US Constitution, abolishing slavery in US; celebrated as National Freedom Day
Operative Bricklayers' Society unionist in London, England start working 8-hour days, instead of 9
Jefferson Long of Georgia is first African American to make an official speech in US House of Representatives (opposing leniency to former Confederates)
The first edition of theatrical newspaper The Stage is published.
US Assay Office in St Louis, Missouri authorized
French Lt-Colonel Gustave Borgnis-Desbordes reaches Bamako on the Niger
First volume of the Oxford English Dictionary, A-Ant, is published
Harvey Wilcox of Ks subdivides 120 acres he owned in Southern California & starts selling it off as a real estate development (Hollywood)
Mrs William Astor invites 400 guests to a grand ball at her mansion thus beginning use of "400" to describe socially elite
Giacomo Puccini's opera "La Boheme" premieres in Turin
1st auto insurance policy in US issued, by Travelers Insurance Company
Shinhan Bank, the oldest bank in South Korea, opens in Seoul.
China's empress Tzu-hsi forbids binding woman's feet
Hermann Sudermanns "Es lebe das Leben" premieres in Berlin
Dutch soccer club ADO Den Haag forms in The Hague; ADO represents the amateur branch of the club
Hungarian premier Count István Tisza resigns
1st federal penitentiary building completed, Leavenworth, Kansas
Dorothy Grey, wife of British Foreign SecretaryEdward Greyis fatally injured in a motor accident
In a high-profile trial American railway heir Harry Kendall Thaw is found not guilty of murdering architect Stanford White on grounds of insanity in a New York court
US Assay Office in Salt Lake City, Utah, opens
US forces withdraw from Cuba after liberal Jose Miguel Gomez becomes president; ensuing political instability will bring a threat of US intervention in 1912
1st British labour exchange opens
Dragoumis government forms in Greece
Chicago White Sox and New York Giants play a 10-inning, 3-3 tie in Cairo, Egypt in an exhibition MLB game; part of special 56-game world tour
Pennsylvania State Board of [motion picture] censors appointed
Tanganyika Railway opens
German Grand Admiral Alfred von Tirpitz announces unrestricted submarine warfare against allied shipping
German spy Lothar Witzke arrested by CIP agent Byron S. Butcher at the US border at Nogales - only German spy sentenced in the US during WWI
Brooklyn Robins trade former NL MVP Jake Daubert to Cincinnati Reds for outfielder Tommy Griffith; result of a salary grievance
1st commercial armored car introduced (St Paul, Minn)
Royal Canadian Mounted Police forms as Royal Northwest Mounted Police merge with Dominion Police
Allied ultimatum on Lithuanian occupation of Memel
Amsterdam's Netherlands Press Museum opens
1st national conference of KPD's Rotfrontkämpferbund in Berlin
Kirghiz Autonomous Region in RSFSR becomes Kirghiz ASSR
Land at Broadway & Wall Street sold at a record $7 per sq inch
"The Broadway Melody" directed by Harry Beaumont and starring Bessie Love is released. 1st film with sound to win an Oscar (Outstanding Picture 1930)
1st clean & jerk of 400 lbs (182 kg), Charles Rigoulet, 402½ lbs
Pi Alphha Phi, the first Asian-American interest fraternity in the United States, is founded at UC Berkeley
Colonial government arrests Anton de Kom in Paramaribo Suriname
Dutch bishops forbid membership of non-catholic unions
Austrian Chancellor Engelbert Dollfuss dissolves all political parties but his own right-wing Fatherland Front (Vaterländische Front)
1st "March of Time" newsreel premieres at the Capitol
James T Farrell finishes his "Studs Lonigan" trilogy
Stapleton, Staten Island, becomes a customs-free port
NBC performs the first inter-city television broadcast from its station in New York City to another in Schenectady, New York by General Electric relay antennas.
Soviet Union begins new offensive against Finland
German occupiers makeVidkun QuislingNorwegian premier
Mussert forms pro-Nazi shadow cabinet in Netherlands
Supreme Soviet increases Soviet republics' autonomy
US 7th Infantry/4th Marine Division lands on Kwajalein/Roi/Namur
US Army arrives at Siegfriedlinie
Republic of Hungary proclaims Zoltán Tildy as its communist president
Aleide de Gasperi forms Italian government of christian-dems & communists
NV United Dutch Fokker's Aircraft established
Finland goes 1-2 in the Nordic combined event at the St. Moritz Winter Olympics; Heikki Hasu takes gold ahead of teammate Martti Huhtala
Johnny Palmer sets a 36-hole PGA Tour scoring record with rounds of 62-64 in the Tucson Open; finishes runner-up by 1 stroke to Skip Alexander who cards tournament record total 264
Palestine Post building in Jerusalem bombed
Urko Kekkonen elected Prime Minister of Finland
-50°F (-46°C), Gavilan, New Mexico (state record)
1st X-ray moving picture process demonstrated
Arms manufacturer Alfred Krupp (43) and 28 other convicted German war criminals granted amnesty
UN condemns People's Republic of China as aggressor in Korea
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site; 1st live television broadcast of such detonation by KTLA in Los Angeles, California at 5:30 AM local time
General strike against French colonial rule in Tunisia
SN Behrman's "Jane" premieres in NYC
"General Electric Theater" premieres on CBS TV; Ronald Reagan later hosts
Dr A de Waal appointed as Netherlands' first female assistant secretary of state
Flooding in Netherlands kills 1,835
WEEK TV channel 25 in Peoria, IL (NBC) begins broadcasting
Dutch soccer club De Graafschap "The County" forms in Doetinchem, Netherlands
Scapino Ballet Studio in Amsterdam destroyed by fire
US TV soap opera "The Secret Storm" premieres on CBS
H. C. Hansen appointed premier of Denmark
Hap Day becomes the first man to serve as Toronto Maple Leafs' captain, coach and general manager when he is appointed to run the famous Canadian NHL club
Hague Daily Newspaper reveals war crimes of Hague mayor Schokking
Hayes Alan Jenkins leads only US sweep of Olympic men's figure skating medals at the Cortina d’Ampezzo Winter Games; Ronald Robertson wins silver with bronze to Jenkins' younger brother, David
WSAV TV channel 3 in Savannah, GA (NBC) begins broadcasting
1st black pilot (PH Young) on a US scheduled passenger airline
Gijsbert of Hall appointed mayor of Amsterdam
MLB club owners accept new 5-year pension plan proposed by the players, but reject request to increase minimum salary from $6,000 to $7,500
"Volare" ("Nel blu dipinto di blu") single released by Domenico Modugno (Grammy Award Record of the Year, Song of the Year 1958)
1st US satellite (Explorer I) launched
Egypt & Syria announce plans to merge into United Arab Republic
Manchester United beats Arsenal, 5-4 at Highbury in the team's last game on British soil, 5 days prior to the plane crash at Munich airport that killed 7 players
Tommy Taylor scores 2 goals and Duncan Edwards 1, in Manchester United's 5-4 win vs Arsenal at Highbury; pair amongst 7 players killed 5 days later when team’s charter plane crashes at Munich airport
WFTV TV channel 9 in Orlando, FL (ABC) begins broadcasting
Heiss sisters go 1-2 in US women's Figure Skating C'ships; defending champion Carol wins from Nancy; David Jenkins wins his third straight men's title
Outfielder Zack Wheat, a Brooklyn Robins favourite of the 1910s and 1920s, is elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame; Wheat hit .317 over 19-year career, and batted .300 or better 14 times
Swiss men vote against voting rights for women
WVUE TV channel 8 in New Orleans, LA (ABC) begins broadcasting
4 students stage 1st civil rights sit-in, at Woolworth's in Greensboro, North Carolina
Extreme right-wing rebels in Algiers surrender
1st full-scale test of US Minuteman ICBM is successful
Australian cricket tail-enders Ken Mackay (62no) and Lindsay Kline (15no) hang on for 100 minutes to earn a famous draw on the final day vs West Indies in 4th Test in Adelaide
MLB's National League releases its first 162-game schedule due to expansion (18 games between opponents)
Nyasaland (now Malawi) becomes self-governing under Hastings Banda
Afobaka Dam is completed on the Suriname River
French sisters Christine Goitschel (gold) and Marielle Goitschel (silver) become first female siblings to win Olympic gold and silver in the same event when they dominate the slalom in Innsbruck
Indiana Governor Mathew Walsh tries to ban "Louie Louie" for obscenity
The Beatles' first #1 hit "I Want to Hold Your Hand," tops the Hot 100, stays #1 for 7 weeks before being replaced by "She Loves You", also by The Beatles
NL adopts emergency team replacement plan to restock any club struck by disaster
Severe bushfires in Tasmania destroy $11 million & take 60 lives
WCLP TV channel 18 in Chatsworth, GA (PBS) begins broadcasting
World trade conference Unctad 2 opens in New Delhi
Tim Wood wins second of 3 straight US Men's Figure Skating titles whilst Janet Lynn takes first of her 5 consecutive Women's National Championships in Seattle
WPGH TV channel 53 in Pittsburgh, PA (IND) begins broadcasting
Stalled commuter train rammed by express in Argentina, 139 die
West-Germany & USSR sign gas contract
WMAA TV channel 29 in Jackson, MS (PBS) begins broadcasting
1972 NFL Draft: Walt Patulski from University of Notre Dame first pick by Buffalo Bills
Kuala Lumpur becomes a city by a royal charter granted by the Yang di-Pertuan Agong of Malaysia.
The first scientific handheld calculator, the HP-35, is introduced for $395
The Ministry of Defence also issues a detailed account of the British Army's version of events during 'Bloody Sunday'
Wings release single "Give Ireland Back to the Irish" in UK
Outfielder Monte Irvin is elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame by the Special Committee on the Negro Leagues; fourth inductee through the committee
Kuala Lumpur is declared a Federal Territory.
"Hoppy, Gene & Me" single by Roy Rogers peaks at #65
Otis Francis Tabler is 1st open homosexual to get security clearance to work for the Defense Department
Edward IIIis crowned King of England aged 14, though the country is ruled by his mother Queen Isabella and her lover Roger Mortimer
Queen Elizabeth Iof England signs the death warrant for her cousin,Mary, Queen of Scots
British sailor Alexander Selkirk is rescued byWilliam Dampierafter being marooned alone on an island in the South Pacific for four years, inspiringDaniel Defoe's "Robinson Crusoe"
The Kalabalik or Tumult in Bender - Ottoman troops of sultan Ahmed III attack camp and seize his unwelcome guest, KingCharles XIIof Sweden
English-German astronomerWilliam Herschelannounces the star Lambda Herculis is close to the solar apex
Lord Byron's "Corsair" sells 10,000 copies on day of publication
Ralph Waldo Emersonand Charles Sumner meet with PresidentAbraham Lincolnat The White House, Washington
Giacomo Puccini's opera "Manon Lescaut" premieres in Turin
Thomas Edisoncompletes the world's first movie studio in West Orange, New Jersey
US Secretary of StateJohn Hayprotests granting Russia exclusive privileges in China, on ground that it runs contrary to the 'open door' policy granting all nations equal rights there
Elihu Root, then Secretary of War, resigns from the cabinet of PresidentWilliam McKinleyand is succeeded byWilliam Howard Taft

Italian tenorEnrico Carusomakes his first recordings in America,  singing "Questa o quella" and "La donna è mobile" fromGiuseppe Verdi's "Rigoletto" in Carnegie Hall, NYC, for Victor Talking Machine Company
American all-round athleteJim Thorpesigns to play baseball with the NY Giants; unlike his other sporting endeavours the Olympic-medal winner's career in MLB was uninspiring (1913-19)
Franz Lehar's opera "Wo die Lerche singt" ("Where The Lark Sings") premieres at the Király Theatre in Budapest, Hungary
Jerome Kern, Guy Bolton &P. G. Wodehouse's musical "Oh Lady! Lady!!", premieres at the Princess Theatre, NYC; runs for 219 performances
Fascists Voluntary Militia forms in Italy underBenito Mussolini
Noël Coward's stage comedy "The Young Idea" premieres at the Savoy Theatre, London; runs for 60 performances
Ramsay MacDonald's incoming Labour government formally recognizes the Soviet Union
Arnold Schoenberg's opera "Von heute auf morgen" ("From Today to Tomorrow") premieres at the Oper Frankfurt in Frankfurt, Germany
German Parliament is dissolved by PresidentPaul von Hindenburgby the request of new chancellorAdolf Hitler

Benny Goodmanand his Orchestra record "And the Angels Sing", a reworking of trumpeter Ziggy Elman's "Fralich in Swing", with Martha Tilton singing lyrics added byJohnny Mercer, for Victor Records in NYC; it becomes a chart topper
Second Norwegian government ofVidkun Quislingforms
Norwegian politicianTrygve Lieelected the 1st Secretary General of the United Nations
Dmitri Shostakovichnamed professor at conservatoire of Leningrad
Important South African anti-apartheid novel "Cry the Beloved Country" byAlan Patonis published in the US
Nine Malay sultanates and two British Straits Settlements (Penang and Malacca) form theFederation of Malaya
Green Bay Packers founder, player and coachCurly Lambeauresigns after 31 seasons and 6 NFL titles to his credit
USSR demands condemnation ofEmperor Hirohitofor war crimes
"You Are There" withWalter Cronkitepremieres on CBS television
Felix Wankel's first working prototype DKM 54 of the rotary Wankel engine was running at the NSU research and development department Versuchsabteilung TX in Germany
Australian Championships Men's Tennis: In a classic all-Australian finalRod LaverbeatsNeale Fraser5-7, 3-6, 6-3, 8-6, 8-6; Laver's first Grand Slam title
British ministerEnoch Powellmakes medical insurance more expensive

John Huston's film drama "The Misfits" officially premieres at the Loews Capitol in NYC, after a sneak preview at Reno, Nevada's Grenada Theatre; the final movie for both Clark Gable andMarilyn Monroewas written by Monroe's husbandArthur Miller, and also featured Montgomery Cliff
DutchQueen Julianaopens Brienenoord Bridge in Rotterdam

Former world heavyweight boxing championFloyd Pattersonbeats Canadian George Chuvalo by unanimous decision in a 12-round non-title clash at New York’s Madison Square Garden; 'The Ring' names bout Fight of the Year.
Martin Luther King Jr.and 700 demonstrators arrested in Selma, Alabama
Peter Jennings, 26, becomes anchor of ABC's nightly news
The 10-team American Basketball Association (ABA) withGeorge Mikanas Commissioner is formed and lasts 9 years; its three-point shot remains a feature of the game
Former US VPRichard Nixonannounces candidacy for president
Vince Lombardisteps down as NFL Green Bay Packers head coach in favor of longtime assistant Phil Bengtson; stays on as Packers' general manager for 1968
In his first start in a Ford, legendary driverRichard Pettywins the Motor Trend 500 at Riverside, California; previous 92 NASCAR victories were in Plymouths
Former MLB CommissionerFord Frickas well as ex-players Earle Combs and Jesse Haines are elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame
Northern Ireland PM Chichester-Clark meets British Home SecretaryJames Callaghanto discuss the economy of Northern Ireland
Rangers' goalieTerry Sawchukrecords his 447th (and final) victory, and 103rd career shutout when New York beats the Pittsburgh Penguins, 6-0; both are NHL records at the time
British Prime MinisterEdward Heathannounces the appointment of Lord Chief Justice Lord Widgery to undertake an inquiry into the 13 deaths on 'Bloody Sunday' (30 January 1972)
Reprise Records releases "Harvest", the 4th studio album byNeil Young; becomes best selling album in US, 1972
KingCarlos Iof Portugal and his heir, Prince Luís Filipe, are assassinated by Republican sympathizers in Terreiro do Paço, Lisbon
Russia adopts the Gregorian calendar (making the day February 14)
Saigon police chief Nguyễn Ngọc Loan executes Viet Cong officer Nguyễn Văn Lém with a pistol shot to the head. The execution is captured by photographer Eddie Adams and becomes an anti-war icon.
Walter de Stapledon, English bishop (1308-26), born in Annery, Devonshire, England (d. 1326)
Conrad Celtes, German humanist scholar, born in Wipfeld, Germany (d. 1508)
Johannes Trithemius, German Benedictine Abbot of Sponheim who amassed a monastery library of 2,000 books, born in Trittenheim, Germany (d. 1516)
Edward Coke, English Chief Justice and politician (defended common law), born in Mileham, Norfolk, England (d. 1634)
Isaac Aboab da Fonseca, Portuguese-Jewish rabbi, scholar and religious writer prominent in Amsterdam and in Dutch Brazil, born in Castro Daire, Portugal (d. 1693)
Gabriel Schütz, German musician, composer, and teacher, born in the Free Imperial City of Lübeck (d. 1710)
Marquard Gude, German archaeologist and classical scholar famous for his collection of Greek and Latin inscriptions, born in Rendsburg, Holstein (d. 1689)
Elkanah Settle, British poet and playwright (The World in the Moon, The Empress of Morocco), born in Dunstable, England (d. 1724)[1]
Jacob Roggeveen, Dutch navigator and explorer (1st European to discover Easter Island), born in Middleburg, Netherlands (d. 1729)
Mother Ignacia del Espiritu Santo, Filipino Religious Sister of the Roman Catholic Church, born in Bonondo, Manila, Philippines (d. 1748)
Miguel Lopez, Spanish composer and organist, born in Villarroya de la Sierra, Aragon, Spain (d. 1723)
Alessandro Marcello, Italian composer, born in Venice, Republic of Venice (d. 1747)
Johann Adam Birkenstock, German composer and violinist, born in Alsfeld, Landgraviate of Hesse-Darmstadt, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1733)
Francesco Maria Veracini, Italian opera composer, born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1768)
Frederick Louis, English prince of Wales and son of George II, born in Hanover, Germany (d. 1751)
Konrad Ernst Ackermann, German actor and theatre director (Die Oberpfalz), born in Schwerin, Germany (d. 1771)
John Philip Kemble, British actor and director (Drury Lane, Covent Garden), born in Prescot, Lancashire, England (d. 1823)
Agustín de Betancourt, Spanish civil engineer (steam engines, hot air balloons), born in Puerto de la Cruz, Tenerife (d. 1824)
Christian Hendrik Persoon, Cape Colony-born pioneering mycologist, born in  Stellenbosch, Cape Colony (d. 1836)
Thomas Campbell, Irish-American clergyman (founder of Disciples of Christ movement in America), born in County Down, Ireland (d. 1854)
Hippolyte-André-Baptiste Chélard, French violist, composer and conductor, born in Paris, France (d. 1861)
Charles-Joseph Sax, Belgian music instrument builder of wind and brass instruments whose son invented the saxophone, born in Dinant, Wallonia, Belgium (d. 1865)
William Dunn Moseley, American politician, 1st Governor of Florida under statehood, born in Lenoir County, North Carolina (d. 1863)
Abraham Emanuel Fröhlich, Swiss poet and fabulist (Ulrich von Hutten, Hundert neue Fabeln), born in Brugg, Switzerland (d. 1865)
Adolf Fredrik Lindblad, Swedish lieder and classical composer, born in Skänninge, Sweden (d. 1878)
Émile Maximilien Paul Littré, French lexicographer, philologist and philosopher, born in Paris, France (d. 1881)[1]
Auguste Blanqui, French revolutionary socialist (Blanquist movement and workers' leader), born in Puget-Théniers, France (d. 1881)
William Bowen Campbell, American politician and Civil War Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Sumner County, Tennessee (d. 1867)
Charles Lenox Remond, African-American orator and abolitionist, born in Salem, Massachusetts (d. 1873)
Henry Lawrence Eustis, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1885)
George Hendric Houghton, American Protestant Episcopal clergyman and founder of the Church of Transfiguration, born in Deerfield, Massachusetts (d. 1897)
Alphonse de Rothschild, French banker, born in Paris, France (d. 1905)
Meyer Guggenheim, Swiss-born patriarch of the Guggenheim family, one of the world's wealthiest families in the 19th century, born in Lengnau, Switzerland (d. 1905)
John Potts Slough, American politician, lawyer and Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1867)
Henry McNeal Turner, American Methodist Bishop and politician, 1st African American army chaplain, born in Newberry, South Carolina (d. 1915)
Emil Hartmann, Danish organist and composer (Towards The Light), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1898)
James A. Herne, American playwright (Hearts of Oak; Margaret Fleming), born in Troy, New York (d. 1901)
William Henry Davenport, American stage magician, claimed to be a spirit medium, born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1877)
Eduard Adolf Strasburger, German botanist who discovered that the nucleus of plant cells divides during cell division, born in Warsaw, Russian Empire (d. 1912)
Granville Stanley Hall, American pioneering psychologist, born in Ashfield, Massachusetts (d. 1924)
Durham White Stevens, American diplomat and advisor to the Korean government (1904-08), born in Washington, D.C (d. 1908)
Victor Herbert, American cellist, composer, and conductor (Babes in Toyland; Eileen), born in Guernsey, Bailiwick of Guernsey (d. 1924) [he believed he was born in Dublin, Ireland][1]
Andrew Kehoe, American mass murderer (Bath School disaster), born in Tecumseh, Michigan (d. 1927)
Jerome F. Donovan, American lawyer, Democratic politician, and US Congress member (1918-21), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1949)[1]
Paul Fort, French poet (founder of Vers et Prose), born in Reims, France (d. 1960)
Clara Butt, British dramatic contralto (Edward Elgar - The Land of Hope and Glory; Sea Pictures), born in Southwick Sussex, England (d. 1936)
John Barry, Irish recipient of the Victoria Cross, born in Kilkenny, Ireland (d. 1901)
Joseph Allard, French Canadian fiddler and composer, born in Lery, Quebec (d. 1947)
Hugo von Hofmannsthal, Austrian poet, dramatist and essayist (founded Salzburg festival), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1929)
Thomas Dunhill, British light classical composer (The Wind Among The Reeds; Tantivy Towers), teacher, and musicologist, born in London, England (d. 1946)
Alfréd Hajós, Hungarian swimmer (Olympic gold 100/200m 1896), soccer forward (1 cap) and manager (Hungary 1906), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1955)
Hattie Wyatt Caraway, American politician (1st woman elected to US Senate, Senator for Arkansas 1931-45), born near Bakerville, Tennessee (d. 1950)[1]
Milan Hodža, Slovak politician and 10th Prime Minister of Czechoslovakia, born in Sučany, Hungary, Austro-Hungarian Empire (d. 1944)[1]
Antonio Guarnieri, Italian cellist and opera conductor (La Scala, 1929-50), born in Venice, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1952)
Francesco Balilla Pratella, Italian composer, born in Lugo, Italy (d. 1955)
José Ignacio Quintón, Puerto-Rican composer (El coquí; Requiem por Angel Mislán), born in Caguas, Puerto Rico (d. 1925)
Tip Snooke, South African cricket all-rounder and captain (26 Tests, 35 wickets), born in St Mark's, Eastern Cape, South Africa (d. 1966)
Yevgeny Vakhtangov, Armenian-Russian theatrical director of the Moscow Art Theatre (Vakhtangov Theatre), born in Vladikavkaz, Russia (d. 1922)
Bradbury Robinson Jr., American football pioneer (threw first legal forward pass at a game at Carroll College in Waukesha, Wisconsin 1906), born in Bellevue, Ohio (d. 1949)
Rosey Rowswell, American MLB broadcaster (Pittsburgh Prates 1936-54), born in Alton, Illinois (d. 1955)
Yevgeny Zamyatin, Russian writer and Soviet dissident (We), born in Lebedyan, Russian Empire (d. 1937)
Camille Chautemps, French politician (Prime Minister of France 1930, 1933-34, 1937-38), born in Paris, France (d. 1963)
Charles Nordhoff, British-American author (The Bounty Trilogy, The Hurricane), born in London (d. 1947)[1][2]
Dirk Roosenburg, Dutch architect (KLM building and design), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1962)
Gertrude Caton-Thompson, British archaeologist (Zimbabwe, So Arabia), born in London, England (d. 1985)
Rudolf Mengelberg, Dutch composer and musicologist (Amsterdam Concert Hall), born in Krefeld, Germany (d. 1959)
James P. Johnson, American pianist and composer ("The Charleston"; "If I Could Be With You (One Hour Tonight)"), born in New Brunswick, New Jersey (d. 1955)
Conn Smythe, Canadian Hockey Hall of Fame team owner (Toronto Maple Leafs 1927-61; Stanley Cup x 8), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1980)
Anastasio Somoza García [Tacho], General, dictator and President of Nicaragua (1937-56), born in San Marcos, Nicaragua (d. 1956)
Frank Lane, American MLB executive (GM Chicago White Sox, St. Louis Cardinals, Cleveland Indians, KC Athletics, Milwaukee Brewers), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1981)
Denise Robins, English romantic novelist (1st Long Kiss), born in London, England (d. 1985)
Al Watrous, American golfer (British Open 1926 runner-up), born in Yonkers, New York (d. 1983)
Clarence "Bud" Pinkston, American diver (Olympic gold 10m platform, silver 3m springboard 1920), born in Wichita, Kansas (d. 1961)
Stephen Potter, English writer and humorist (School for Scoundrels, Shipbuilders), born in London, England (d. 1969)
Carlo Borbolla, Cuban pianist, organist, and composer, born in Manzanillo, Cuba (d. 1990)
Therese Brandl, German Nazi concentration camp guard (Auschwitz), and convicted war criminal, born in Staudach-Egerndach, Bavaria, German Empire (d. 1947, by execution)

Carl Reynolds, American baseball outfielder, 1927-39 (Chicago White Sox, Boston Red Sox, and 3 other tems) who was 2nd player in MLB history to hit HRs in 3 consecutive innings (1930), born in LaRue, Texas (d. 1978)
Georg Rendl, Austrian writer (The Bees Novel), beekeeper and painter, born in Zell am See, Austria (d. 1972)
S J Perelman, American author, humorist and screenwriter (The New Yorker, Around the World in 80 Days), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1979)
Ernst Stueckelberg, Swiss mathematician and physicist, born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1984)
Helen Chandler, American actress (Christopher Strong, Dracula), born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1965)
Hildegarde [Sell], American cabaret vocalist (“Leave It To The Girls"; "Darling, Je Vous Aime Beaucoup"), born in Adell, Wisconsin (d. 2005)
Pierre Capdevielle, French conductor, composer (Élégie de Duino), music critic (Monde musicale), and teacher, born in Paris (d. 1969)
(Mozart) Camargo Guarnieri, Brazilian composer, pedagogue, and conductor, born in Tietê, São Paulo (d. 1993)
Alan Strode Campbell Ross, British linguist who coined the terms "U" and "non-U" as class indicators in language usage, birthplace unknown (d. 1980)[1]
John Canaday, American art critic (Metropolitan Museum of Art Portfolios), born in Fort Scott, Kansas (d. 1985)
Albie Booth, American College Football Hall of Fame halfback (Yale University), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1959)
Edward Staempfli, Swiss pianist, conductor, and composer, born in Bern, Switzerland (d. 2002)
Gene Sheldon, American actor (Zorro - "Bernardo"), mime artist, and banjo player, born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 1982)
George Pal, Hungarian-American film director (When Worlds Collide, Puppetoons), born in Cegléd, Hungary (d. 1980)
Douglas Hall, 14th Baronet, British colonial administrator (last British Governor of Somaliland Protectorate 1959-60), born in England (d. 2004)
George Beverly Shea, Canadian-American Grammy Award-winning gospel singer (Billy Graham Crusades, 1947-2013), born in Winchester, Ontario (d. 2013)
Jahangir Khan, Indian cricket fast bowler (4 Tests India; Pakistan selector), born in Jalandhar, Punjab, India (d. 1988)
Michael Kanin, American director, writer and actor (Woman of the Year, Teacher's Pet), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1993)
Jeffrey Kindersley Quill, British test pilot (Spitfire), born in Littlehampton, Sussex (d. 1996)
A K Hangal, Indian freedom fighter and Bollywood character actor (Aaina, Sholay), born in Sialkot, Punjab, British India (d. 2012)
Gus Tinsley, American College Football Hall of Fame end and coach (All-American 1935, 36; coach LSU 1948-55; NFL: Chicago Cardinals), born in Ruple, Louisiana (d. 2002)
Mary Alicia Rhett, American actress and portrait painter (Gone with the Wind), born in Savannah, Georgia (d. 2014)
Stanley Matthews, English soccer forward (54 caps; Stoke City, Blackpool; 1st British player to be knighted), born in Stoke-on-Trent, England (d. 2000)
Bruce Gordon, American actor (The Untouchables), born in Fitchburg, Massachusetts (d. 2011)
Gordon Hobday, English scientist (developed penicillin with Alexander Fleming, worked on Ibuprofen), born in New Sawley, Derbyshire (d. 2015)
